issues.title: Documentation trap when using -bind in conjunction with (automatically configured) Tor hidden services
issues.body:
To start with the conclusion: everything appears to work as _intended_, so this should probably not be considered a bug as such, but some logging and telemetry output could be improved to make things clearer.

If you use -bind to manually specify which (public) IPs to listen to, Bitcoin Core will also no longer create the (local) default socket for incoming Tor connections at 127.0.01:8334 unless you specify it manually in your list - this is probably working as intended. When automatically creating a Tor hidden service, it will instead be routed to the public IPv4 port, and incoming Tor connections still work just fine.

However, without the dedicated Tor socket, Bitcoin Core is unable to distinguish between regular IPv4 connections and Tor connections, so all of the latter will be counted as the former.

The specific case that had me scratching my head for a bit: -netinfo reported zero incoming Tor connections

```
         ipv4    ipv6   onion   total   block
in        291      28       0     319
out         5       3       2      10       2
total     296      31       2     329
```

while the true number of incoming Tor connections was 29.

```
ss -tupan | grep tor | grep 8333 | wc -l
29
```

As expected, removing the -bind statements from the config and restarting fixed it.

```
         ipv4    ipv6   onion   total   block
in         25       4       5      34
out         4       1       5      10       2
total      29       5      10      44
```

While the documentation for -bind hints towards why this is the case, Bitcoin Core gives no indication in the logs or the -netinfo output that the configuration is suboptimal, and I feel like maybe it should do that just to save people (like me) from getting confused.

Maybe during startup, if no Tor socket is created and a Tor hidden service is created, it should add something like "Warning: -bind was specified but lacks a dedicated Tor socket; incoming Tor connections will be counted as regular connections", and when using -netinfo it should show a "?" or "-" instead of "0".

comment_data.body:
> I think the right approach might not be a warning. Since`-listenonion`defaults to` true`, we start `StartTorControl` by default. If we are still accepting incoming Tor connections, my suggestion is that we should try to make the count correct for `-netinfo`.
> 
> Two possible solutions come to mind(I have not tested any, so I don't know if they are accurate) Option 1 Override `-listenonion` If `-bind `is specified and no `=onion` is in the bind list, automatically set` listenonion=0`: No Tor hidden service created User gets exactly what their` -bind` implies `-netinfo` gets correct count
> 
> Option 2 Enforce `=onion `binding If -bind is specified and `listenonion=true`, and `no =onion `is in the bind list, automatically add `127.0.0.1:8334=onion:` Tor socket created `inbound_onion=true` -netinfo Count is accurate

On second thought, this suggestions would not work. listenonion and -bind should not be coupled.

comment_data.body:
> No clear indication what the problem might be. Any way to resolve this issue, please?

Bitcoin Core makes heavy use of CPU, RAM and storage IO. Hardware defects might only become visible when running Bitcoin Core. You might want to check your hardware for defects.

* Use software such as memtest86 to check your RAM.
* Use software such as linpack, or Prime95 to check the CPU behaviour under load.
* Use software such as smartctl, fsck, badblocks, or CrystalDiskInfo to test your storage device use.

Source: https://bitcoin.stackexchange.com/a/12206

Usually the issue tracker is used to track technical issues related to the Bitcoin Core code base.

General bitcoin questions and/or support requests are best directed to the [Bitcoin StackExchange](https://bitcoin.stackexchange.com) or the `#bitcoin` IRC channel on Libera Chat, or one of the Bitcoin subreddits, or any other place that you feel is well suited.

> It took me quite some time to understand that a manual cleanup was required. At the very least, the log message should clearly explain the nature of the problem and suggest a recovery path, such as running with -reindex or deleting the index directory.
> 
> Automatic reindexing may be controversial, but the current error handling feels too opaque for non-developers running full nodes.


I guess it could make sense to adjust the error message to mention that a single reindex may be considered. However, if the issue persists, then a reindex every time does not seem helpful and a software or hardware fault is most likely, which can only be fixed by replacing the software or hardware.

issues.title: crash during IPC cleanup after `interruptWait()` is called
issues.body:
while working on https://github.com/stratum-mining/sv2-apps/pull/177, I'm noticing a new crash

steps to reproduce:
- run Bitcoin Core `v30.2` on `mainnet`
- `git clone https://github.com/plebhash/sv2-apps -b 2026-01-09-bitcoin-core-v30-2`
- edit `sv2-apps/pool-apps/pool/config-examples/mainnet/pool-config-bitcoin-core-ipc-example.toml` so that `unix_socket_path` field points to `node.sock`
- launch Pool via: `cd sv2-apps/pool-apps/pool; cargo run -- -c config-examples/mainnet/pool-config-bitcoin-core-ipc-example.toml`
- let Pool run for a while
- kill Pool with ctrl+c

this does not happen deterministically, so a few attempts of launching then killing Pool might be needed 

but eventually Bitcoin Core crashes with a few different log variations:

comment_data.body: Note the bug reported here (crash after disconnecting during a waitNext call that returns a non-null pointer) was actually reported earlier in https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/issues/33554#issuecomment-3378637474 and https://github.com/bitcoin-core/libmultiprocess/issues/226, but I didn't notice the connection until now. The bug was fixed in   https://github.com/bitcoin-core/libmultiprocess/commit/0174450ca2e95a4bd1f22e4fd38d83b1d432ac1f from https://github.com/bitcoin-core/libmultiprocess/pull/240 and https://github.com/bitcoin/bitcoin/pull/34422
